title: Optical Pulling Force in Carbon Nanotubes: Manifestation of Nonlocal Conductivity
authors: Tomer Berghaus, Touvia Miloh, Gregory Ya. Slepyan
abstract: We develop a new theory of an optical force exerted on a carbon nanotube (CNT) with a nonlocal conductivity. The optical force is expressed in terms of the surface current density and the axial electric field on the CNT surface. To determine these quantities, we employ an integral-equation-based approach in terms of the current density. The analysis is constructed for a finite-length cylindrical CNT by rigorously accounting for edge effects. In addition to numerical solutions of the integral equation, we obtain an approximate analytical expression for the optical force acting on the CNT, which shows good agreement with numerical simulations. We also demonstrate the existence of some frequency ranges in which the optical force becomes negative, corresponding to the optical pulling effect. Such a pulling behavior is shown to originate from the nonlocality of the conductivity and to vanish in the local limit. This work advances theoretical understanding of optomechanical interactions in finite-length low-dimensional conductors and clarifies the role of spatial dispersion in the emergence of optical pulling forces.
summary: This paper provides a new theory for the optical forces acting on carbon nanotubes, specifically highlighting how nonlocal conductivity enables an 'optical pulling' effect. By accounting for edge effects and spatial dispersion, the authors show that the force can become negative, offering new insights into the optomechanical control of nanotubes.
main_problem: Developing a theoretical framework to describe the optical force on finite-length carbon nanotubes (CNTs) by accounting for nonlocal conductivity and edge effects.
main_result: The study demonstrates that nonlocality in conductivity leads to an 'optical pulling' effect (negative axial force) in certain frequency regimes, a phenomenon that vanishes in the local conductivity limit.
method: The authors employ an integral-equation-based approach using the Maxwell Stress Tensor and a Fredholm integral equation of the second kind to solve for surface current density and electric fields.
model_or_system: A finite-length, cylindrical, zigzag carbon nanotube (m, 0) modeled as a 1D conductor using a pseudo-spin electron liquid model and the Kubo formalism.
key_observables: Axial component of the optical force (pushing vs. pulling), surface current density, and axial electric field.
important_parameters: Frequency (THz to visible), electrochemical potential (mu), CNT length (L), radius (R), and the nonlocality parameter (xi).
main_assumptions: Low temperature (collision-free electron motion), zero-temperature approximation, and a Taylor series expansion of spatial dispersion truncated at the first-order term.
figures_summary: Fig 1: Schematic of CNT under oblique wave incidence; Fig 2: Nonlocal parameter and conductivity vs frequency; Fig 3: Optical force comparison (numerical, analytical, local, non-local); Fig 4: Force dependence on electrochemical potential; Fig 5: Force dependence on CNT length in different frequency regimes.
paper_structure: The paper begins with the derivation of nonlocal conductivity from a Hamiltonian/Liouville approach, moves to the construction of the electromagnetic integral equations for the Hertz vector, presents numerical and analytical solutions for the optical force, and concludes with a comparison of local vs. non-local regimes.
why_interesting: The discovery of an optical pulling force arising from spatial dispersion is highly relevant to quantum optics and optomechanics, as it provides a mechanism for light-induced manipulation of low-dimensional nanostructures.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
